figures.caption: Figure 1: Examples of continuous (a) and discontinuous (b) constituent trees, and different linearization strategies (1-4) for encoding those trees. SH = Shift, SH#k = Shift#k, NT−X = Non-Terminal-X, RE = Reduce, RE#k−X = Reduce#k-X, SW = Swap, and SW#k = Swap#k.

Figure 2: Transition sequence used by a transition-based parser to construct the discontinuous tree shown in Figure 1(b), employing the in-order + Swap transition system along with the buffer and stack data structures. NT−X = Non-Terminal-X.

Figure 3: Transition sequence used by a transition-based parser to construct the discontinuous tree shown in Figure 1(b), employing the in-order + Shift#k transition system along with the buffer and stack data structures. NT−X = Non-Terminal-X.
Table 1: Architecture and optimizer hyper-parameters for each pre-trained encoderdecoder model.
Figure 4: F-score achieved by continuous linearizations on BARTLarge across structural factors and sentence lengths.

Figure 5: F-score of the lexicalized in-order linearization across pre-trained encoder–decoder models (including the encoder-only model with the non-lexicalized in-order strategy by Fernández-González and Gómez-Rodríguez [15] as “F&G2023”) with respect to structural factors and sentence length.
Figure 6: F-score of lexicalized and non-lexicalized discontinuous linearizations on BARTLarge/mBART and [15] (F&G2023) by structural factors and sentence length.

Table 2: F-score comparison of state-of-the-art constituent parsers on the PTB test set, without the use of gold or predicted PoS tags. The second section of the table is dedicated exclusively to sequence-to-sequence models. Additionally, we report parser performance on the PTB development set for our models. The abbreviation F&R [15] refers to work by Fernández-González and Gómez-Rodríguez [15], and we report the performance obtained by both non-lexicalized top-down and in-order linearizations. Parsers leveraging additional dependency information are marked with +dep; those that combine multiple trained models through ensembling are denoted +ensemble and models using language models to rerank predicted trees are labeled +LM-rerank. Models using predicted PoS tags as auxiliary input are annotated with +PoS. Finally, systems that initialize the encoder with pre-trained language models such as BERTLarge [24], RoBERTaLarge [25] or XLNetlarge [49] are labeled +BERTLarge/+RoBERTaLarge/+XLNetlarge.

Table 3: F-score and Discontinuous F-score (DF1) comparison of leading discontinuous constituent parsers on the TIGER, NEGRA, and DPTB test sets, without the use of gold or predicted PoS tags. R&M [58] refers to the model by Ruprecht and Mörbitz [58], and F&R denotes a series of works by Fernández-González and Gómez-Rodríguez. In particular, for the sequence-to-sequence approach introduced by Fernández-González and GómezRodríguez [15], we examine non-lexicalized linearization variants. We adopt the same abbreviations as in Table 2 to indicate additional enhancements of parsers. Additionally, a model initialized with the German pre-trained language model GottBERTbase [60] is labeled as +GottBERTbase, and the notation +BERTX is used when the specific size of the BERT model is unspecified. For English, only the BARTlarge variant is employed in our models. Finally, we mark the best results for task-specific and sequence-to-sequence parsers in bold, while italic indicates the best results among our lexicalized linearizations.
